solution: The integrand's odd-power terms \( ([[a3]])x^{3} \) and \( ([[a1]])x \) integrate to \( 0 \) over the symmetric interval \( [-[[m]], [[m]]] \). Only the even part contributes: \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} \left(([[a2]])x^{2} + ([[a0]])\right)dx = 2\left(\tfrac{[[a2]][[m]]^{3}}{3} + [[a0]][[m]]\right) = [[answer]] \).

stem: Every term of \( f(x) = ([[a5]])x^{5} + ([[a3]])x^{3} + ([[a1]])x \) is an odd power of \(x\), so \(f\) is an odd function. Using symmetry (not by finding an antiderivative), evaluate \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} f(x)\,dx \).
solution: Every term of \(f\) has an odd power of \(x\), so \(f(-x) = -f(x)\); an odd function's signed area over a symmetric interval \([-[[m]], [[m]]]\) always cancels. Hence \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} f(x)\,dx = [[answer]] \), with no antiderivative needed.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats \(f\) as even and doubles the half-interval integral.

An odd function's signed area over the symmetric interval \([-[[m]],[[m]]]\) cancels exactly, so \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} \dfrac{[[b]]x}{x^{2}+[[a]]}\,dx = [[answer]] \).

stem: The functions \( f(x) = x \) and \( g(x) = ([[a]])x^{3} + ([[b]])x \) are both odd. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} f(x)\,g(x)\,dx \).

So \(f(x)g(x) = ([[a]])x^{4}+([[b]])x^{2}\) is even, and \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} f(x)g(x)\,dx = 2\int_{0}^{[[m]]} \left(([[a]])x^{4}+([[b]])x^{2}\right)dx = 2\left(\tfrac{[[a]][[m]]^{5}}{5}+\tfrac{[[b]][[m]]^{3}}{3}\right) = [[answer]] \).

stem: Evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[p]]}^{[[p]]+[[m]]} \dfrac{e^{x}}{e^{x}+e^{2[[p]]+[[m]]-x}}\,dx \) using the reflection substitution \( x \mapsto (2[[p]]+[[m]])-x \), which swaps the two exponential terms in the denominator, rather than computing a logarithmic antiderivative directly.
solution: Let \(I\) be the integral and reflect via \(x \mapsto (2[[p]]+[[m]])-x\): the integrand maps to \( \dfrac{e^{2[[p]]+[[m]]-x}}{e^{2[[p]]+[[m]]-x}+e^{x}} = 1 - \dfrac{e^{x}}{e^{x}+e^{2[[p]]+[[m]]-x}} \), and the bounds are unchanged (reversed then flipped back). So the reflected integral equals \( [[m]] - I \), but it also equals \(I\) (same region), giving \(2I = [[m]]\) and \(I = [[answer]]\), independent of the exponential's exact form.

stem: A particle moves along a line so that its velocity at time \(t\) seconds is \( v(t) = ([[a]])t^{3} + ([[b]])t \) meters/second. Since \(v\) is an odd function of \(t\), find the particle's net displacement (in meters) from \( t=-[[m]] \) to \( t=[[m]] \).
solution: Because \(v(-t) = -v(t)\), the particle's motion from \(t=-[[m]]\) to \(0\) exactly reverses its motion from \(0\) to \(t=[[m]]\), so the net displacement is \( \int_{-[[m]]}^{[[m]]} v(t)\,dt = [[answer]] \) meters.
